topic.what: Randomized controlled trial evaluating the efficacy of Rikkunshito compared to control for relieving functional dyspepsia symptoms, specifically epigastric pain and postprandial fullness.
topic.how: Double-arm RCT with standardized symptom scoring instruments measuring patient-reported outcomes at baseline and follow-up intervals after Rikkunshito administration vs. control.
topic.why_matters: Provides level-1 clinical evidence for Rikkunshito efficacy in functional dyspepsia, supporting its integration into gastroenterology guidelines and demonstrating that traditional Kampo formulas can meet modern evidence standards.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Change in patient-reported epigastric pain score from baseline after Rikkunshito treatment
characteristics.vs_baseline: Statistically significant reduction in pain scores vs. control group
characteristics.context: Epigastric pain syndrome subtype of functional dyspepsia; Japanese outpatient population

characteristics.what_it_measures: Change in patient-reported postprandial fullness/distress score from baseline
characteristics.context: Postprandial distress syndrome subtype; consistent with prokinetic mechanism of action

characteristics.what_it_measures: Composite functional dyspepsia symptom burden across pain, fullness, nausea dimensions
characteristics.vs_baseline: Improved vs. control on composite endpoint
characteristics.context: Composite endpoint consistent with clinically meaningful response

good_for: Adult patients with diagnosed functional dyspepsia (Rome criteria)
Patients with postprandial distress syndrome or epigastric pain syndrome subtypes
Clinical settings with access to standardized Kampo preparations
Guideline development requiring RCT-level evidence for Rikkunshito
not_for: Organic gastrointestinal diseases (peptic ulcer, GERD, malignancy)
Patients with contraindications to Kampo herbal formulations
requires: Confirmed functional dyspepsia diagnosis by Rome criteria
Access to standardized Rikkunshito extract granules (TJ-43 or equivalent)
Baseline symptom assessment using validated instruments

novelty: One of the first RCTs demonstrating statistically significant Rikkunshito efficacy for functional dyspepsia with validated symptom instruments
Establishes clinical evidence base that directly informed subsequent Japanese gastroenterology guideline recommendations
Demonstrates feasibility of applying conventional RCT methodology to traditional Kampo formulas
open_problems: Sample size may limit subgroup analyses by functional dyspepsia subtype
Blinding integrity not fully characterized (herbal odor/taste may unblind participants)
Long-term durability of symptom improvement beyond trial period unknown
Generalizability to non-Japanese populations with different dietary habits untested
